content:
# Disagreement Log — Joshua 5
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Total Disagreements:** 20

---

### D-1 | Verse 5:1 | "spirit" vs. "fight"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there was no longer spirit in them" | וְלֹא-הָיָה בָם עוֹד רוּחַ |
| LOGOS | "there was no longer any spirit in them" | same |
| PNEUMA | "They had no fight left in them" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"there was no longer spirit in them"** | |
**Rationale:** Ruach encompasses breath, spirit, resolve, and will — not merely "fight." The broader term preserves the Hebrew's semantic range. PNEUMA's rendering is vivid but reductive. EMET/LOGOS alignment adopted.

---

### D-2 | Verse 5:1 | "to the west" vs. "to the sea"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to the sea" | יָמָּה |
| LOGOS | "to the west" | same |
| PNEUMA | "west of the Jordan" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to the west"** | |
**Rationale:** Yamah functions here as a directional indicator ("seaward" = "westward"). "To the sea" is etymologically accurate but potentially misleading. "To the west" conveys the intended geographical meaning. Three-way split; LOGOS adopted.

---

### D-3 | Verse 5:2 | "flint knives" vs. "knives of flint"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "knives of flint" | חַרְבוֹת צֻרִים |
| LOGOS | "flint knives" | same |
| PNEUMA | "flint knives" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"flint knives"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (LOGOS/PNEUMA). English attributive construction is the standard handling of Hebrew construct chains. No meaning is lost.

---

### D-4 | Verse 5:2 | "again, a second time" vs. "again"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "return, circumcise ... a second time" | וְשׁוּב מֹל ... שֵׁנִית |
| LOGOS | "circumcise ... again, a second time" | same |
| PNEUMA | "circumcise ... again" | same (omits שֵׁנִית) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"circumcise ... again, a second time"** | |
**Rationale:** Both shuv and shenit are present in the Leningrad Codex. The double emphasis is deliberate. PNEUMA's omission loses a word that is in the source text.

---

### D-5 | Verse 5:4 | "came out" vs. "went out"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who went out from Egypt" | הַיֹּצֵא מִמִּצְרַיִם |
| LOGOS | "who came out of Egypt" | same |
| PNEUMA | "who had come out of Egypt" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"who came out of Egypt"** | |
**Rationale:** Yatsa can be "go out" or "come out." From the narrative vantage in Canaan, "came out" is more natural. Two-to-one (LOGOS/PNEUMA). Minor divergence.

---

### D-6 | Verse 5:6 | "to give to us" vs. "to give us"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to give to us" | לָתֶת-לָנוּ |
| LOGOS | "to give us" | same |
| PNEUMA | (paraphrased away) | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to give to us"** | |
**Rationale:** The first-person plural lanu is a striking narrative voice shift. "To give to us" preserves this emphasis more explicitly. EMET adopted despite being the minority — textual fidelity to this unusual grammatical feature takes precedence.

---

### D-7 | Verse 5:7 | Word order — "them Joshua circumcised"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "them Joshua circumcised" | אֹתָם מָל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ |
| LOGOS | "these Joshua circumcised" | same |
| PNEUMA | "whom Joshua circumcised" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"them Joshua circumcised"** | |
**Rationale:** Three-way split. The Hebrew fronts the object pronoun for emphasis — these sons, not the fathers. EMET's rendering preserves this marked word order most faithfully. Emphatic fronting is adopted for the consensus.

---

### D-8 | Verse 5:8 | "recovered" vs. "were healed" vs. "had healed"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "until they recovered" | עַד חֲיוֹתָם |
| LOGOS | "until they were healed" | same |
| PNEUMA | "until they had healed" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"until they were healed"** | |
**Rationale:** Three-way split. Chayah here means "to be restored to health." "Were healed" captures the medical sense of recovery from circumcision. LOGOS adopted.

---

### D-9 | Verse 5:9 | "reproach" vs. "disgrace"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the reproach of Egypt" | חֶרְפַּת מִצְרַיִם |
| LOGOS | "the reproach of Egypt" | same |
| PNEUMA | "the disgrace of Egypt" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the reproach of Egypt"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Cherpah denotes a shame imposed from outside — a taunt, a scorn. "Reproach" preserves this external accusatory quality. "Disgrace" leans toward self-inflicted shame in English.

---

### D-10 | Verse 5:9 | Etiological formula rendering
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And he called the name of that place Gilgal until this day" | וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא גִּלְגָּל עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה |
| LOGOS | "So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day" | same |
| PNEUMA | "And so that place was called Gilgal — and it is called that to this day" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"So the name of that place has been called Gilgal to this day"** | |
**Rationale:** Three-way split. LOGOS's rendering captures both the etiological naming and the persistence of the name in idiomatic English. EMET's "And he called" leaves the subject ambiguous. PNEUMA adds words not in the Hebrew.

---

### D-11 | Verse 5:10 | "kept" vs. "made" vs. "celebrated"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they made the Passover" | וַיַּעֲשׂוּ אֶת-הַפֶּסַח |
| LOGOS | "they kept the Passover" | same |
| PNEUMA | "they celebrated the Passover" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they kept the Passover"** | |
**Rationale:** Three-way split. Asah with Pesach is a fixed idiom for Passover observance. "Kept" is the traditional English rendering for festival observance. "Made" is too literal; "celebrated" adds festive connotation not necessarily in the Hebrew.

---

### D-12 | Verse 5:11 | "parched grain" vs. "roasted grain"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "parched grain" | קָלוּי |
| LOGOS | "parched grain" | same |
| PNEUMA | "roasted grain" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"parched grain"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Qalui refers to grain dried and roasted over flame — "parched" is the standard biblical rendering and more precise than the generic "roasted."

---

### D-13 | Verse 5:12 | "ceased" vs. "stopped"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the manna ceased" | וַיִּשְׁבֹּת הַמָּן |
| LOGOS | "the manna ceased" | same |
| PNEUMA | "the manna stopped" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the manna ceased"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Shavat carries the resonance of sabbath-rest and completion. "Ceased" preserves this gravitas. "Stopped" implies abruptness and loses the shavat overtone.

---

### D-14 | Verse 5:13 | "behold" vs. "look" vs. (omitted)
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and look" | וְהִנֵּה |
| LOGOS | "and behold" | same |
| PNEUMA | (omitted — integrated into narrative) | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"and behold"** | |
**Rationale:** Three-way split. Hinneh is a narrative presentative signaling surprise. It must be rendered, not absorbed. "Behold" preserves the heightened register appropriate to a theophanic encounter.

---

### D-15 | Verse 5:13 | "for our adversaries" vs. "on theirs"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "or for our adversaries" | אִם-לְצָרֵינוּ |
| LOGOS | "or for our adversaries" | same |
| PNEUMA | "or on theirs" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"or for our adversaries"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Tsarenu ("our adversaries") is a concrete noun. "On theirs" loses the word entirely and weakens the binary whose refusal gives verse 14 its theological force.

---

### D-16 | Verse 5:14 | "No" vs. "Neither"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "No" | לֹא |
| LOGOS | "No" | same |
| PNEUMA | "Neither" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"No"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Lo is a single stark syllable — the hardest negative in Hebrew. "Neither" is interpretive and softens the refusal. The Hebrew does not say "neither" (lo zeh v'lo zeh); it says "No." The abruptness is theologically essential — the commander refuses the binary itself.

---

### D-17 | Verse 5:14 | "bowed down" vs. "in reverence"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bowed down" | וַיִּשְׁתָּחוּ |
| LOGOS | "bowed down" | same |
| PNEUMA | "in reverence" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"bowed down"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Vayyishtachu describes a physical action — prostration to the ground. The Hebrew narrates what the body does. "In reverence" substitutes motive for action.

---

### D-18 | Verse 5:14 | "What does my lord speak" vs. "What does my lord wish to say"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What does my lord speak to his servant?" | מָה אֲדֹנִי מְדַבֵּר אֶל-עַבְדּוֹ |
| LOGOS | "What does my lord speak to his servant?" | same |
| PNEUMA | "What does my lord wish to say to his servant?" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"What does my lord speak to his servant?"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). Medabber is a participle meaning "speaking." PNEUMA's "wish to say" adds a volitional element not present in the Hebrew text.

---

### D-19 | Verse 5:15 | Full prepositional phrase vs. abbreviated
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" | שַׁל-נַעַלְךָ מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ |
| LOGOS | "Remove your sandal from upon your foot" | same |
| PNEUMA | "Take off your sandal" | same (omits מֵעַל רַגְלֶךָ) |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Remove your sandal from upon your foot"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (EMET/LOGOS). The phrase me'al raglekha is in the Hebrew and preserves the significant singular number (foot, not feet), distinguishing this from Exodus 3:5. Omitting it loses a textual datum critical for intertextual comparison.

---

### D-20 | Verse 5:15 | "is holy" vs. "holy it is"
| Translator | Rendering | Hebrew |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "holy it is" | קֹדֶשׁ הוּא |
| LOGOS | "is holy" | same |
| PNEUMA | "is holy" | same |
| **SOPHIA** | **"is holy"** | |
**Rationale:** Two-to-one (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The predicate-fronted Hebrew construction (qodesh hu) is a standard pattern. While EMET's preservation of Hebrew word order is valuable for a literal translation, the consensus text normalizes to English word order without loss of meaning.

---

### Summary Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Total disagreements logged | 20 |
| EMET/LOGOS agreement adopted | 12 |
| LOGOS adopted (three-way split) | 4 |
| EMET adopted (minority, textual fidelity) | 2 |
| LOGOS/PNEUMA agreement adopted | 2 |
| PNEUMA adopted | 0 |

**Pattern:** PNEUMA's dynamic renderings were consistently overridden in favor of closer fidelity to the Masoretic Text. In every case where EMET and LOGOS agreed, the council adopted their shared reading. In three-way splits, the council favored the rendering that best balanced fidelity to the Hebrew with natural English. In two cases (D-6, D-7), the council adopted EMET's minority reading because textual fidelity to unusual Hebrew features (first-person narrator voice, emphatic fronting) outweighed the majority.

---

*Signed: SOPHIA (Σοφία — Wisdom)*
*Arbiter of the SOPHIA Bible*
